coredata.dc:title: Differentiation and determination of metal ions using fluorescent sensor array based on carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yapei
Liu, Xue
Wu, Qiuhua
Yi, Jie
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: In chemical and biological sensing research, design and preparation of various sensors is a question of first importance. Generally, fabrication of specific sensors always requirs amounts of time-consuming and low-efficient synthetic tasks. Herein, a novel sensor array based on carbon nanodots (CDs) was constructed to differentiate and detect metal ions (including Ag+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, and Pb2+), which utilized non-specific collective recognition reactions between CDs and various metal ions. The convenient synthetic methods and abundant carbon source greatly simplified the constructing process of the sensor array. Moreover, the hexa-sensor array can be simplified into binary-sensor array using principal component analysis (PCA) method. The binary-sensor array can be constructed in less than 10min with competitive working performance as before. The binary-sensor array has its most comfort pH zone of around 5–10 and can perform well in the metal ion concentration of 50–1600μM, suitable for the differentiation and determination of metal ions. The sensor array can also realize qualitative detection of unknown metal ions under the interference of a specific environment, including fetal calf serum and local running water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor array
Fluorescence
Carbon nanodots
Principal component analysis
Metal ions analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The construction as well as the working mechanism of the CD sensor array. (A) Fluorescence responses of the sensor array towards six kinds of metal ions (IA, IB, IC, ID, IE and IF); (B) Fingerprints (response patterns) of various metal ions generated by the sensor array; (C) Discrimination of unknown metal ion through Mahalanobis distance analysis (MDA) using principal component analysis (PCA) scattergraph.
PCA scattergraph from original sensor array and simplified sensor array. The original sensor array contains 7 sensors, while the simplified sensor array contains only 2 sensors (CD3 and CD5).

The Influence of different substances environment on sensor array (A) The influence of water with Li+, Mg2+, Sr2+ and K+ on sensor array. (B) The influence of drink with vitamin B, vitamin C and glucose on sensor array.

(A) PCA scattergraph for the response of the sensor array at different pH values. 6 data points with the same colour represent 6 repeated experiments carried out in the same pH environment. (B) PCA scattergraph for the response of the sensor array with six metal ions at different concentrations. 6 data points with the same colour represent 6 metal ions with the same concentration.

Identification of unknown metal ions through the simplified sensor array in practical environmental simulation. MI (metal ion) 1–4 are unknown metal ions randomly chosen from the original six metal ions. (A) MI 1–2 were detected in fetal calf serum, (B) while MI 3–4 were detected in the local tap water.

head.ce:title: Differentiation and determination of metal ions using fluorescent sensor array based on carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In chemical and biological sensing research, design and preparation of various sensors is a question of first importance. Generally, fabrication of specific sensors always requirs amounts of time-consuming and low-efficient synthetic tasks. Herein, a novel sensor array based on carbon nanodots (CDs) was constructed to differentiate and detect metal ions (including Ag+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, and Pb2+), which utilized non-specific collective recognition reactions between CDs and various metal ions. The convenient synthetic methods and abundant carbon source greatly simplified the constructing process of the sensor array. Moreover, the hexa-sensor array can be simplified into binary-sensor array using principal component analysis (PCA) method. The binary-sensor array can be constructed in less than 10min with competitive working performance as before. The binary-sensor array has its most comfort pH zone of around 5–10 and can perform well in the metal ion concentration of 50–1600μM, suitable for the differentiation and determination of metal ions. The sensor array can also realize qualitative detection of unknown metal ions under the interference of a specific environment, including fetal calf serum and local running water.

ce:section.ce:para: The design and construction of highly selective chemical or biological sensors is always time-consuming and low-efficient. Inspired by our olfactory and gustatory systems [1,2], cross-reactive sensor arrays have attracted increasing interest [3–5]. Sensor arrays usually contain a series of nonselective sensors, and analytes can be differentiated and recognized through the cumulative non-specific responses from all the sensors [3–5]. Interestingly, using specific data processing method, such as principle component analysis (PCA) [6], the dimensionality of sensor array can be decreased, and a simplified sensor array with fewer sensors can work none the better for it [7].

Nowadays, various sensing materials have been applied to construct sensor arrays, including inorganic materials (metal oxides [8], semiconductor nanocrystals [9], etc), organic materials (fluorescent dyes [10], conductive polymers [11], molecularly imprinted polymers [12], etc) and nanomaterials [13,14]. However, synthesis for these sensing materials is time-consuming, especially for sensor arrays containing a set of sensors. Therefore, developing new sensing materials with simple synthetic method and excellent sensing performance can promote the development of sensor arrays. Carbon nanodots (CDs) are one of the most important and attractive carbon materials [15–17]. CDs can emit stable fluorescence and have already displayed encouraging performance in chemical sensing [18–21] and biological sensing [22–25]. It is worth noting that CDs can be prepared from extensive and low-cost carbon sources using simple and convenient methods, including microwave-assisted or solvothermal methods. These merits make CDs very ideal candidates for sensor array application.

Heavy metal accumulation can cause serious environmental and health issues [26]. Among various techniques to detect heavy metals, optical methods (via fluorescence changes or colorimetric changes) are the most convenient methods, along with high sensitivity and low detection limit [27,28]. Hydrophilic fluorescent CDs possess abundant functional groups with oxygen-nitrogen, which endow CDs with combining capacity with metal ions [29]. Through fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) or electron/energy transfer effect, the fluorescence intensity of CDs will change quantitatively, making CDs candidates as sensors for various metal ions [30–35]. Recently, there have been several reports about applying CDs as sensor units to construct sensor arrays to differentiate metal ions [36,37].

Here, we selected seven kinds of CDs and realized differentiation for six kinds of metal ions. Differing from previous reports, we took PCA to process the resulting data and succeeded to decrease the dimensionality of the sensor array from six to two units, which is less than the existing sensor array based on CDs. Moreover, the sensing performance of the two-sensor array was as excellent as before. The construction and working mechanism of the sensor array is shown in Scheme 1 . For each metal ion, there exists a unique fingerprint or response pattern (Scheme 1B), containing all fluorescence intensity changing information from CD array (Scheme 1A). PCA was applied to process the response pattern including six repeated trails for each metal ions, and the first two principal components (PCs, describing at least 80% of the total variability) were taken as the horizontal and vertical coordinates to plot PCA scattergraph (Scheme 1C). Based on the PCA scattergraph, different metal ions can be differentiated, and unknown metal ions can be determined through Mahalanobis distance analysis (MDA).

In this paper, seven carbon nanodots (CDs) were prepared through microwave and hydrothermal methods. CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4 and CD5 were prepared through microwave reaction. CD6 and CD7 were prepared through hydrothermal reaction.

CD1 was synthesized following a typical procedure. 0.6g asparaginic acid (4.5mmol) and 1.2g urea (20.0mmol) were dissolved in NaOH solution (15mL, 0.5M) before the solution was heated in the microwave oven for 6min. The resulted solid powder was dissolved with 10mL ultrapure water. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12 000rpm for 5min and then dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 48h to remove the excessive precursors and small molecules. The resultant CD1 was maintained at 4°C for further use.

0.5g alanine (5.6mmol) and 1.2g urea (20.0mmol) were dissolved in NaOH solution (15mL, 0.5M) and then the solution was heated in the microwave oven for 4min. The resulted solid powder was processed using the same method as CD1.

CD3 was synthesized using 0.3g lysine (2.3mmol) and 1.7g ethylenediamine (28.3mmol) in ultrapure water (10mL). The mixture solution was heated in the microwave oven for 4min. The following processing method was similar to CD1.

CD4 was synthesized using 0.6g alanine (4.5mmol) and 1.0g citric acid (5.2mmol) in NaOH solution (15mL, 0.5M). The mixture solution was heated in the microwave oven for 6min, and the following processing method was similar to CD1.
CD5 was synthesized using 1.0g citric acid (5.2mmol), 0.13g cysteine (1.0mmol) and 1.0g urea (1.7mmol) in ultrapure water (10mL). The reaction and following processing method were similar to CD1.

CD6 was synthesized using 0.6g cytosine (1.0mmol) in NaOH solution (10mL, 0.5M). The solution was transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled down naturally. The following processing method was similar to CD1.

CD7 was synthesized using 0.4g critic acid (2.2mmol) and 0.5g ethylenediamine (8.3mmol) was dissolved in ultrapure water (10mL). The reaction was carried out in a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200°C for 5h. The following processing method was similar to CD6.
The fluorescence quantum yields of seven CDs measured in these studies can be found in the Supplementary Information.

The characterization information of carbon nanodots was presented in the supporting information (Fig. S1 and Table S1 in ESI). In the presence of metal ions, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of CDs was unshifted under the same excitation wavelength. The D-value (difference value) was obtained by comparing the normalized fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of metal ion solution. For each metal ion, the collective D-values constituted its specific figureprint (Fig. S2 in ESI). The D-value (difference value) of fluorescence intensity was processed through normalization, and the fingerprint of each metal ion was obtained (Scheme 1B), which contained all the information that seven CDs responded to such metal ion. From these fingerprints, we also got some important information about the sensor array. For example, all the CDs possess carboxyl groups and show fine binding capability with Fe3+, affecting on the quenching degree of the CDs. Judging from the fingerprint of Cr2+, CD6 is the best sensor for Cr2+, and CD2 is the most insensitive sensor for Cr2+. The sensing mechanism of CDs on various metal ions is still a matter of current debate and requires further clarification. There are several explanations for this, such as fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) or electron/energy transfer during ions binding process [38–40].

The multi-dimensional response pattern (7 sensors×6 metal ions×6 trails) of the sensor array in the presence of six metal ions (Ag+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, and Pb2+) was statistically analysed using PCA method. PCA can convert the original fluorescence intensity variables in the fingerprints into a new set of linearly uncorrelated principal components (PCs) [41]. According to the Kaiser rule, PCs with eigenvalues greater than one have statistical significance [42]. Therefore, only the first PC (PC 1, eigenvalue=5.588) and the second PC (PC 2, eigenvalue=1.049) were listed in Fig. 1 A. Herein, PC 1 accounts for 79.83% of the variability in the data, while the succeeding second component (PC 2) has 14.98% variance. The first two PCs contributes ca. 95% of the total variability, and therefore they were applied to plot the PCA scattergraph (Fig. 1B). No obvious clustering is observed in the scattergraph, which indicates that the PCA scattergraph can differentiate six metal ions clearly. For one metal ion, all data points from six repeated trials are close to each other, which are marked using a circle, representing an exclusive zone for such metal ion. The high level of data dispersion in the PCA scattergraph can be attributed to the high cross-reactivity given by the CD receptors, while the specificity due to the unique fluorescence variation generated by the sensor-ion interaction.

The ultimate goal in sensor array research is to use less sensors for a better sensing performance. One of the most important applications for PCA is to decrease the data dimensionality, which can be used to simplify the sensor array [43]. Based on our above-mentioned description, PC 1 and PC 2 were chosen as the principal components of the sensor array. Their correlation formula was shown in the supporting information, and factor scores were listed in Fig. 1A. Factor scores can act as important parameters to evaluate the contribution of each individual sensor to the construction of PC. The ideal sensors would be the ones contributing the most for each individual PC with statistical significance [44]. Therefore, from Fig. 1A, we can see that, compared with the other sensors, sensor CD5 contributes the most (47.06%) to PC 1, while sensor CD3 contributes the most (71.76%) to PC 2. Combining the fact that PC 1 and PC 2 are the principal components of the sensor array, we can speculate that CD3 and CD5 are the most important sensors of all. Therefore, in order to simplify the original sensor array, we reconstructed the sensor array only using sensor CD3 and CD5, and the PCA scattergraph is shown in Fig. 1C. The new PCA scattergraph still displays clustering with no clear overlap between all of the samples, which effectively confirms that the simplification of the sensor array has not affected its sensing performance. Moreover, both CD3 and CD5 were prepared through simple and convenient microwave-assisted method only in a domestic microwave oven. It cost just less than ten minutes to construct the sensor array with a satisfactory performance.

After that, the interference from other metal ions and substance if they are present in the detection system were texted. We choose the environment of water rich in Li+, Mg2+, Sr2+, K+ and other metals, and drink with vitamin B, vitamin C and glucose. The sensor array of dimensionality reduction still has good separation ability in these two environments (Fig. 2 ). Metal ions and substances in the environment do not affect the sensor array.

The above-mentioned sensing experiments for metal ions were carried out in neutral environment. However, for practical application, the sensing performance of various sensors is always affected by environmental factors, such as pH value. CDs have been reported to be sensitive to pH and can even function as a pH probe [45,46] Therefore, it is necessary to study the performance of the sensor array at various pH values. The simplified sensor array constructed by only CD3 and CD5 was investigated. The D-value of fluorescence intensity for these two CDs at various pH values were collected and processed to form fingerprints related to pH values (Fig. S3in ESI). PCA was applied to analyze the resulted data, and the PCA scattergraph for the response of the sensor array to various pH values Fig. 3 A. The PCA scattergraph can be divided into four clusters, including pH 1–3, pH 4, pH 5–10 and pH 11–14. At low pH value (pH<4), the protonation of the binding sites of the sensors can affect their interaction with metal ions, further influencing their sensing performance for metal ions. On the other hand, at a higher pH value (pH>8), besides the deprotonation influence for the sensors themselves, pH environment can also induce precipitation of metal salts or hydroxides [47]. Therefore, pH 5–7 is the most “comfort zone” for metal ion sensing research, in which the sensing performance of the sensor array should be paid more attention to. Within the pH range from 5 to 7, the PCA scattergraph shows an obvious overlap between all the samples, which demonstrates that such pH range has a weak effect on the performance of the sensor array.

The concentration of metal ions can also affect the sensing performance of the sensor array. The simplified sensor array was further investigated in eight typical concentrations below 2mM. At a given concentration, there was a series of fingerprints related to various metal ions (Fig. S4 in ESI). Eight sets of PC score data were generated from PCA processing on the data at each concentration and plotted in one PCA scattergraph (Fig. 3B). Two obvious clusters can be seen in the PCA scattergraph, corresponding to the concentration of 4 and 16μM respectively. This demonstrates that the sensor array cannot discriminate between different metal ions effectively in the concentration range of 0–16μM, indicative of their limits of detection (LOD). With increased metal ion concentration, the data points with the same concentration present a more dispersive distribution, indicating a good performance for the sensor array in such concentration range (50–1600μM).

The potential practical application of the sensor array to identify the unknown metal ions is significant. Hence, double blind experiments were carried out to evaluate the potention of the simplified sensor array for indentifying unknown metal ions. Two kinds of typical environments were chosen as the background environments, including fetal calf serum solution and local tap water solution. Four kinds of metal ions were randomly chosen from the original six metal ions and detected in these two environments respectively (Fig. 4 ). Their normalized D-values of fluorescence intensity were substituted into the correlate equation generated during the PCA process of the simplified sensor array. The resulted data were plotted in the PCA scattergraph (Fig. 4A and 4B hollow points). According to Mahalanobis distance analysis (MDA), the data points of MI 1 are close to those owing to Cd2+, which indicates that MI 1 is Cd2+. In a similar way, MI 2–4 can be judged as Fe3+, Pb2+ and blank solution, respectively. These results demonstrate that the as-prepared sensor array can be applied to identify unknown metal ions, which have contributed to the previous fingerprints. At the same time, the accuracy of the sensor array to determine an unknown metal ion is almost unaffected in the testing environment. That means that the simplified sensor array can simultaneously realize the differentiation and identification of various metal ions in practical application.

Herein, a novel kind of sensor array based on CD sensors was designed and constructed to differentiate and determine metal ions. The sensor array made use of the collective response of a set of CDs towards various metal ions. Using PCA, the sensor array can be simplified to two-sensor array without decreasing the sensing accuracy. The simplified sensor array perform well in the ion concentration range of 50–1600μM, as well as in the comfort pH zone for metal ion sensing research (pH 5–7). The simplified sensor array can also realize the qualitative detection of unknown metal ions in a specific environment. Combining the merits of CDs with simple synthetic methods and abundant preparing source, the design and construction of the sensor array is more convenient and easier. It cost less than ten minutes to construct the simplified sensor array. Based on the above results, we can conclude that the as-prepared sensor array has wide application prospect in practical application. However, for further practical application, we should expand the current sensor array to differentiate and determine more metal ions with fewer sensor unites.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51403093 and 51373073).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.132.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

A practical approach to optical cross-reactive sensor arrays
The use of principal component analysis and discriminant analysis in differential sensing routines
Kinetically controlled chemical sensing using micromachined structures
Selective gas nanosensors with multisize CdSeCdSe nanocrystal/polymer composite films and dynamic pattern recognition
Combinatorial approach to organelle-targeted fluorescent library based on the styryl scaffold
Chemical sensors based on amplifying fluorescent conjugated polymers
Water-compatible molecularly imprinted polymers obtained via high-throughput synthesis and experimental design
Gold nanoparticle–fluorophore complexes: sensitive and discerning noses for biosystems sensing
Array-based sensing with nanoparticles: ‘Chemical noses’ for sensing biomolecules and cell surfaces
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Instant visual detection of trinitrotoluene particulates on various surfaces by ratiometric fluorescence of dual-emission quantum dots hybrid
Photoluminescent graphene oxide ink to print sensors onto microporous membranes for versatile visualization bioassays
Graphene oxide embedded sandwich nanostructures for enhanced Raman readout and their applications in pesticide monitoring
In situ growth of surfactant-free gold nanoparticles on nitrogen-Doped graphene quantum dots for electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide in biological environments
Real-time discrimination and versatile profiling of spontaneous ROS in living organisms with a single fluorescent probe
A chemically reactive Raman probe for ultrasensitively monitoring and imaging the in vivo generation of femtomolar oxidative species as induced by anti-tumor drugs in living cells
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-Based fluorescent probe for the sensitive turn-on detection of glutathione and its cellular imaging
Photochemical deposition of surface-clean silver nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive colorimetric detection of glutathione
Removal of heavy metal ions from wastewaters: a review
Fluoro- and chromogenic chemodosimeters for heavy metal ion detection in solution and biospecimens
Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for detection of lead, cadmium, and mercury ions
Carbon quantum dots-based nanoprobes for metal ions detection
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Fluorescence detection of mercury ions and cysteine based on magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots and implication logic gate operation
Nitrogen and phosphorus Co-doped carbon nanodots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in human serum and living cells
Microwave synthesis of carbon dots with multi-response using denatured proteins as carbon source
One-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence
Fluorescence sensor array based on amino acid derived carbon dots for pattern-based detection of toxic metal ions
Excited oxidized-carbon nanodots induced by ozone from low-temperature plasma to initiate strong chemiluminescence for fast discrimination of metal ions

Reusable sensor based on functionalized carbon dots for the detection of silver nanoparticles in cosmetics via inner filter effect

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The eigenvalues-greater-than-one rule and the reliability of components
Rational design of a minimal size sensor array for metal ion detection
Selection of adsorbates for chemical sensor arrays by pattern recognition
Carbon dot-based inorganic–organic nano system for two-photon imaging and biosensing of pH variation in living cells and tissues
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-Color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Fluorescence sensor array for metal ion detection based on various coordination chemistries: general performance and potential application

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yapei Wu is working for his MS degree from the Department of chemistry, majoring in Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Liaoning University. He is interested in the design sensor arrays and preparation of carbon nanomaterials.

Xue Liu is an associate professor at the College of chemistry, Liaoning University, China. He received her PhD degree from the college of chemistry, NanKai University, China in 2013. Now, he is engaged in the preparation and application of the carbon nanomaterials.

Qiuhua Wu is an associate professor in College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China. She received hPhD degree from College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China in 2016. Now, she is engaged in the simulation of the biomineralization.
Jie Yi is working for his PhD degree, majoring in organic Chemistry, in Liaoning University. He focuses on preparation and application of the carbon nanomaterials.

Guolin Zhang is a professor in College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China. He received his PhD degree from College of Chemistry, Nankai University, China in 2004. Now, he is engaged in Janus materials, carbon nanomaterials and sensors.
